ground_truth.title: Scaling Go Testing with Contract and Scenario Mocks
ground_truth.author: Preetam Jinka
ground_truth.main_content:
Mocks aren't the enemy; bad tests are. We scale Go testing using "Tactical Pairs": Contract Tests for truth, Scenario Mocks for logic. At FunnelStory, we manage a complex distributed system that orchestrates LLMs, syncs CRM data, and handles long-running workflows. In this environment, relying solely on "real" integration tests left us with blind spots. We couldn't reliably test what happens when an API sends a 429 Rate Limit, or when a workflow needs to pause for exactly 24 hours. More importantly, we realized that the majority of our edge cases depend on specific combinations of integrations that exist in a customer's workspace—states that are impossible to reproduce or recreate in our own environments.

When testing systems, you face two opposing risks: Mock Drift (mocks are static, but reality evolves) and the Integration Trap (testing logic that depends on external volatility is slow, flaky, and non-deterministic). The solution isn't to pick a side; it's to sequence them. You should never mock a dependency until you have verified its behavior with a real, isolated Contract Test.

A Contract Test verifies the Data Layer against real infrastructure. It does not test business logic; it tests the Round Trip. By writing data and immediately reading it back, we prove that the code speaks the correct SQL dialect and that the struct tags match the schema. For external APIs, we verify our client can parse Real JSON Responses using httpmock with a fixture captured from a real Salesforce call.

You cannot simply add mocks to coupled code. The application must define dependencies via interfaces rather than concrete structs. By defining contracts, we separate infrastructure (SQL, HTTP) from business value (filtering, alerting). This approach uses Contract Tests to prove the code interacts with the database and external APIs correctly, and Scenario Tests to prove the code handles business logic and errors correctly. This separation allows for high confidence in the system's behavior without the overhead of maintaining a full end-to-end test suite for every edge case.
with: Mocks aren't the enemy; bad tests are. We scale Go testing using "Tactical Pairs": Contract Tests for truth, Scenario Mocks for logic.
The solution isn't to pick a side; it's to sequence them. You should never mock a dependency until you have verified its behavior with a real, isolated Contract Test.
A Contract Test verifies the Data Layer against real infrastructure. It does not test business logic; it tests the Round Trip.
With trusted contracts in place, we can use mocks to test "Impossible" scenarios—edge cases that are difficult or destructive to reproduce in a real environment.
By defining contracts, we separate infrastructure (SQL, HTTP) from business value (filtering, alerting).
without: Awarded as the top customer success solution by Winning by Design - 2025
